narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know you've likely faced unique challenges and triumphs on your path here, and we're sorry to deliver this tough news about your application being rejected. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated a 66.6% chance that you would not pass, which is high enough to trigger an automatic denial to protect both you and the school's outcomes. This prediction stems from a close look at key parts of your profile, like your academic deciles from early and later years in school, your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, family income level, and whether you'd study full-time. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your lower decile ranking based on Year 3 grades, which stood out well below the typical student's position and signaled potential struggles in sustaining strong performance. Your undergraduate GPA actually worked in your favor, as it was close to average and nudged the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass. Next in influence came your Year 1 decile, also on the lower side compared to most applicants, which added to concerns about early academic momentum. Your LSAT score, notably below the dataset average, further tipped the scales toward predicting failure, despite your full-time study plans and low family income bracket both offering some positive counterbalance—though their impact was smaller. Overall, compared to typical students who get approved, your profile showed weaker spots in grades and test scores that outweighed these strengths, pushing you into the higher-risk category for bar failure. We truly appreciate the resilience it takes to pursue law as a Black woman, and this isn't a reflection of your potential or worth—it's just one model's data-driven call. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted courses or tutoring, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your background more deeply. You have every right to keep pushing; many in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact areas. We're rooting for you—reach out if you'd like resources for next steps.
